data.title: Bitcoin Rises to One-Week High Before U.S. Jobs Data — Market Talk
data.description:
0750 GMT - Bitcoin rises to its highest in one week ahead of key U.S. jobs data at 1230 GMT as markets bet on the Federal Reserve resuming interest-rate cuts from the September 17 meeting. Weaker-than-expected ADP private payrolls data Thursday fuelled expectations for another soft nonfarm payroll report on Friday. This would strengthen the case for the Fed to cut rates later this month and likely beyond, potentially boosting risky assets including bitcoin. Bitcoin rises 2.1% to one-week high of $112,876, LSEG data show, extending gains after breaking back above $112,000. However, it remains well below the record high of $124,480 reached in mid-August, having recently fallen as traders took profits. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0747 GMT - The Indonesian rupiah could receive support from expectations for Fed rate cuts later this month, which is expected to renew broad-based weakness in the dollar, UOB's global economics and markets research team writes in a report. The rupiah has depreciated by 1.5% over the past two months, largely due to consecutive rate cuts by Bank Indonesia, UOB says. BI's dovish stance has weighed on the rupiah, making it one of the region's underperformers. The rupiah could also be weighed by negative sentiment in the near term from recent public protests, UOB says. BI's firm commitment to maintain currency stability is likely to help contain volatility. USD/IDR is little changed at 16,415, LSEG data shows. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0739 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, fall along with their eurozone peers ahead of key U.S. non-farm payrolls data due at 1230 GMT. Weak jobs data could increase expectations for U.S. interest-rate cuts in the coming months, starting in September. They come after U.S. job openings data and ADP private payrolls data came in weaker than expected. "A reading around consensus would be a sign that the U.S. labor market is slowing down sharply," XTB's Kathleen Brooks says in a note. Gilts shrug off U.K. retail sales data showing a solid monthly rise during July but a weaker-than-expected annual increase. Ten-year gilt yields fall 3 basis points to last trade at 4.707%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0719 GMT - Jefferies' bigger worry about France is about ratings rather than about politics. Fitch Ratings is due to review France on Sept. 12, and it already has a negative outlook. "If political uncertainty persists, we see a case for Fitch downgrading France," Jefferies' global economist Mohit Kumar says in a note. However, Moody's Ratings or S&P Global Ratings are unlikely to downgrade France, according to Jefferies. "Risk remains that if France loses the coveted AA level rating (currently AA-) from two or more rating agencies, it could lead to forced selling from some of the Asian accounts." For now, Jefferies remains bearish on French bonds and expects the 10-year French-German bond-yield spread to rise towards 90 basis points, from 77 basis points currently, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0702 GMT - Sterling edges higher after data showed U.K. retail sales rose slightly more than expected during July. Retail sales increased 0.6% in July compared to the previous month, above the 0.5% forecast by economists in a WSJ survey. The data come after Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey said earlier in the week that the timing of future interest-rate cuts is now more uncertain amid concerns about inflationary pressures. The market is pricing in a 36% chance of another rate cut by year-end, LSEG data show. Sterling rises to an intraday high of $1.3471 after the data, from $1.3460 beforehand. The euro falls to an intraday low of 0.8662, pounds from 0.8673 previously. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0637 GMT - Nordic markets are seen opening slightly higher, with IG calling the OMXS30 up 0.3% at around 2615. The market is eagerly awaiting today's U.S. labor market data and how this will affect expectations of the Fed's interest-rate cuts, SEB's chief emerging markets strategist Erik Meyersson writes. SEB expects another month of employment growth of 75,000--in line with consensus--and unemployment to continue to rise slightly. "We think this will be enough for a 25 basis-point cut from the Fed in September." The money market is now pricing in at least two interest-rate cuts by the Fed before the end of the year, he says. OMXS30 closed at 2607.54, OMXN40 at 2293.02 and OBX at 1556.28. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0633 GMT - Eurozone government bond yields fall, tracking the move of U.S. Treasury yields and reflecting expctations of a further cooling U.S. labor market. U.S. payrolls are due at 1230 GMT, with analysts forecasting the addition of 75,000 jobs in August. Weak data is expected to cement interest-rate cut expectations by the Federal Reserve this month. "A September rate cut is likely a done deal," says J.P.Morgan Asset Management's Max McKechnie in a note. "Today's jobs report is more about the Federal Reserve's path from there," the global market strategist says. Eurozone 10-year bond yields drop by up to 4 basis points, with the 10-year German Bund yield falling 2.3 basis points to 2.701%, according to Tradeweb. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield falls 1 basis point to 3.580%. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0630 GMT - The dollar falls as markets await the highly-anticipated U.S. nonfarm payrolls report later for hints on the potential pace of expected Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts. Economists in a WSJ survey expect the report at 1230 GMT to show payrolls rose 75,000 in August. The unemployment rate is expected to rise to 4.3% in August from 4.2% previously and wage growth is forecast to slow to 3.8% year-on-year. With immigration slowing, modest jobs growth doesn't necessarily indicate a loosening labor market, J.P. Morgan Asset Management strategist Max McKechnie says in a note. "Instead, investors should focus on the unemployment rate and wage growth for a clearer sense of the Fed's next steps." The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.113. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0625 GMT - The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield "wants" to have a go at 4% amid expectations of a weak payroll outcome, ING's Padhraic Garvey and Benjamin Schroeder say in a note. August payrolls data due at 1230 GMT, could fortify expectations of a Federal Reserve interest-rate cut this month. "The 10-year [yield] looks to have 4% as a level to aim for, depending on the payrolls number," the rates strategists say. However, a job addition data below 50,000 would likely to be needed to generate that type of market reaction, they say. "The closer to zero [in job additions] the more likely a 4% test becomes," they say. Analysts in a WSJ poll expect 75,000 jobs added in August. The 10-year yield falls 2.3 basis points to 4.152%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0605 GMT - A Trump executive order locks in a 15% base tariff rate for Japan, clearing some uncertainty but leaving key questions unanswered, says Stefan Angrick at Moody's Analytics. The order settles some doubts over car duties and cements Japan's pledge to buy more U.S. rice. But investment provisions remain murky, the economist says. Japan will funnel $550 billion into the U.S., though the composition is unclear. U.S. Commerce Secretary Lutnick insists it's real funding, while Tokyo has said the figure includes loans, guarantees, and equity via government-backed institutions, with direct equity accounting for just 1% to 2%, Angrick says. Economically, tariffs remain punishingly high. They are undercutting manufacturing and exports, and will trim at least half a percentage point from GDP. Politically, the deal offers scant relief for beleaguered PM Ishiba. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0557 GMT - Southeast Asia is likely to continue taking a large share of global foreign direct investment flows, says HSBC economists in a commentary. While the Trump administration's demand for investment commitments from its trading partners could pull flows away from Southeast Asia, the region's economic competitiveness and Chinese businesses' need to diversify should still underpin the strong FDI flows, they say. They expect Southeast Asian economies to keep pursuing free-trade agreements, and their growing consumer markets and digital economies could offer investment opportunities beyond manufacturing. The momentum of outbound investment from China should also remain robust, with Southeast Asia likely to benefit, partly due to the region's geographic significance in China's Belt and Road Initiative, they add. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0556 GMT - The European Central Bank's staff projections at next week's policy meeting will factor in the recent trade deal with the U.S., but revisions are likely to be small versus the June forecasts, SEB Research analysts say in a note. The ECB is currently in no hurry to cut interest rates. December is expected to be the next checkpoint for a possible rate cut, with additional data and another fresh set of staff projections available by then, the analysts say. Money markets expect the ECB to keep interest rates on hold next week, leaving the deposit rate at 2.00%, according to LSEG data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Sofina slips as FX impact hits investments value in H1
data.description:
** Sofina's shares are down 2%, trailing at the bottom of Belgium's BEL20 (.BFX) index, after the holding company's net asset value declined in H1

** H1 NAV stands at 9.8 billion euros, down from 10.3 billion a year ago

** Sofina's CEO blames currency headwinds for the drop, mainly dollar exposure

** Portfolio companies are doing rather well despite the U.S. weakness dampening NAV, says ING analyst Hans D'Haese

** "Most recent transactions point to increasing exposure to financials in Europe vs. the US," he says

** However, with the US dollar relatively flat so far, the FX impact is expected to be neutral in H2, says Degroof Petercam while reiterating its "hold" rating on the shares

** Sofina shares were up 17.9% this year as of Thursday's close

data.title: ASML Holdings Target Raised to EUR750 From EUR660 by UBS
ASML Holdings Raised to Buy From Neutral by UBS
FTSE 100 Extends Gains for Third Session
data.description:
The FTSE 100 rose 0.2% to around 9,200 on Friday, marking a third consecutive gain, as investors digested fresh economic data while awaiting the US jobs report that could shape expectations for a potential Fed rate cut later this month.

UK retail sales volumes climbed 0.6% month-on-month in July, beating forecasts of a 0.2% increase, while separate data from Halifax showed house prices rising 2.2% annually in August, above expectations of 2%.

Analysts noted the figures indicate the UK economy entered the third quarter with “decent momentum,” though speculation about tax increases in the November budget may prompt households to curb spending.

Among individual stocks, early top performers included HSBC Holdings (+1.2%), Rio Tinto (+1.6%), Glencore (+1.6%), and Anglo American (+2%).

For the week, the benchmark index is on track for modest gains.

data.title: Zelenskiy to meet EU's Costa, Slovakia's PM on energy issues
data.description:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said he would meet European Council President Antonio Costa and Slovak Prime Minister Robert Fico in Kyiv on Friday to discuss energy issues.

Ukraine's deputy energy minister Roman Andarak said in Copenhagen on Friday that Zelenskiy was expected to discuss a phase-out of Russian oil deliveries via Ukraine with Fico during their meeting.

Slovakia is heavily reliant on supplies of oil from Russia via the Druzhba pipeline, whose infrastructure Ukrainian drones have attacked recently, causing repeated disruptions in supply that elicited a furious reaction from Bratislava.

Ukraine, battling a full-scale Russian invasion since February 2022, has repeatedly called on other countries to stop buying Russian oil to deprive Moscow of funds to wage its war.

data.description: The Indonesian rupiah could receive support from expectations for Fed rate cuts later this month, which is expected to renew broad-based weakness in the dollar, UOB's global economics and markets research team writes in a report. The rupiah has depreciated by 1.5% over the past two months, largely due to consecutive rate cuts by Bank Indonesia, UOB says. BI's dovish stance has weighed on the rupiah, making it one of the region's underperformers. The rupiah could also be weighed by negative sentiment in the near term from recent public protests, UOB says. BI's firm commitment to maintain currency stability is likely to help contain volatility. USD/IDR is little changed at 16,415, LSEG data shows. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: China solar stocks rally on hopes of an industry turnaround
data.description:
China solar stocks rallied in Friday trading after a major polysilicon producer said the industry had hit bottom and regulators called for more measures to improve sector revenue.

Polysilicon is used to make the silicon wafers that convert sunlight into energy.

The stock price rally came amid intense focus on the government's campaign to reign in excessive, unsustainable competition among Chinese firms to increase prices in the solar sector.

Shares of polysilicon producer GCL Technology Holdings closed up more than 10% on Friday. Solar producer Trina Solar was up over 9% and Tongwei more than 6%.

The companies did not respond to requests for comment.

The chief financial officer of polysilicon giant Daqo New Energy Corp said in a Bloomberg TV interview on Thursday that the polysilicon sector has already hit bottom and is recovering. He added that a plan to purchase and shut down over 1 million tons of the industry's capacity is moving forward with the support of the government.

The company should start to generate profitability before the end of the year, he said.

Adding to positive sentiment for the stocks was a plan issued by China's industry ministry targeting annual average revenue growth of at least 5% for the solar industry.

data.title: Iron ore heads for second weekly gain as softer dollar overshadows demand woes
data.description:
Iron ore futures rose for a fourth straight session on Friday and were heading for a second weekly gain, as a softer dollar and higher bets of a U.S. interest rate cut overshadowed muted demand recovery in top consumer China after a military parade.

The benchmark October iron ore (SZZFV5) on the Singapore Exchange erased earlier loss to trade 0.28% higher at $105.1 a metric ton, as of 0814 GMT.

It has gained 1.6% so far this week, partly supported by a soft dollar amid bets of a U.S. Federal Reserve rate cut.

The most-traded January iron ore contract on China's Dalian Commodity Exchange (DCE) closed daytime trade 0.77% higher at 789.5 yuan ($110.37) a ton, recording a weekly rise of 0.3%.

The price rally in the coal market was ascribed by some analysts as drivers for the ore price strength the afternoon.

Coking coal NYMEX:ACT1! and coke (DCJcv1), other steelmaking ingredients, surged 6.3% and 4.7%, respectively, aided by renewed concerns over supply reduction.

Earlier in the morning session, both ore contracts retreated from multi-week high in the prior day as a sharper-than-expected fall in demand weighed.

Average daily hot metal output, a gauge of iron ore demand, slid by 4.7% from the prior week to 2.29 million tons in the week as of September 4, the lowest since February 28, data from consultancy Mysteel showed.

Market participants had expected a sharper output fall this week due to the production curbs in the top steelmaking hub, Tangshan, for the military parade on September 3 to commemorate the end of World War Two, but a fall of nearly 5% hit some traders and analysts by surprise.

Growing iron ore supply also limited the upside room for prices.

Steel benchmarks on the Shanghai Futures Exchange mostly gained ground on higher raw materials costs.

Rebar , hot-rolled coil and wire rod (SWRcv1) climbed 1%, while stainless steel shed 0.3%.

($1 = 7.1529 Chinese yuan)

data.title: U.S. Futures, European Stocks Mixed
data.description:
U.S. futures for the S&P 500 were up 0.2% and futures for the Dow Jones Industrial Average were flat. Changes in futures do not necessarily predict movements after the opening bell.

In Europe, the Stoxx Europe 600 climbed 0.1% in morning trading. Hexagon Series B rose 6.2% and Bridgepoint Group added 4.8%. On the other hand, Temenos slipped 13.6%, and DKSH Holding dropped 3.8%. The FTSE 100 was flat. Other stocks in Europe were mixed as France's CAC 40 declined 0.2% and Germany's DAX climbed 0.1%.

The Wall Street Journal Dollar Index fell 0.1% to 95.37.

In commodities, Brent crude fell 0.4% to $66.74 a barrel, and WTI crude dropped 0.4% to $63.21 a barrel. The European benchmark price for natural gas, the Dutch futures contract TTF, was up 0.1% to 32.45 euros a megawatt hour.

The German 10-year Bund yield fell by 1 basis point to 2.716% from 2.722%, and the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury was flat at 4.166%. Bond prices and yields move in opposite directions.

Stocks in Asia were up as Japan's Nikkei 225 index gained 1%, and Hong Kong's Hang Seng climbed 1.4%. China's benchmark Shanghai Composite gained 1.2%.

-This article was automatically generated by MarketWatch.

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0750 GMT - Bitcoin rises to its highest in one week ahead of key U.S. jobs data at 1230 GMT as markets bet on the Federal Reserve resuming interest-rate cuts from the September 17 meeting. Weaker-than-expected ADP private payrolls data Thursday fuelled expectations for another soft nonfarm payroll report on Friday. This would strengthen the case for the Fed to cut rates later this month and likely beyond, potentially boosting risky assets including bitcoin. Bitcoin rises 2.1% to one-week high of $112,876, LSEG data show, extending gains after breaking back above $112,000. However, it remains well below the record high of $124,480 reached in mid-August, having recently fallen as traders took profits. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0747 GMT - The Indonesian rupiah could receive support from expectations for Fed rate cuts later this month, which is expected to renew broad-based weakness in the dollar, UOB's global economics and markets research team writes in a report. The rupiah has depreciated by 1.5% over the past two months, largely due to consecutive rate cuts by Bank Indonesia, UOB says. BI's dovish stance has weighed on the rupiah, making it one of the region's underperformers. The rupiah could also be weighed by negative sentiment in the near term from recent public protests, UOB says. BI's firm commitment to maintain currency stability is likely to help contain volatility. USD/IDR is little changed at 16,415, LSEG data shows. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0742 GMT - Gold prices edge higher in early trade after pausing from a record-breaking rally as traders locked in profits ahead of the release of key U.S. nonfarm payrolls data. Futures are up 0.1% to $3,610.40 a troy ounce and on track for a 2.6% weekly gain, after hitting a new high of $3,640.10/oz earlier this week. The precious metal's surge was largely driven by expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates at its next meeting, concerns about the U.S. central bank's independence following President Trump's move to fire Fed governor Lisa Cook, and persistent geopolitical risks. Now, "the biggest risk from a market perspective is an upside surprise to today's payrolls number," says Kathleen Brooks, research director at XTB. "If rate cut bets get scaled back, then inflation fears could ease, which may hurt the world's most famous inflation hedge." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0739 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, fall along with their eurozone peers ahead of key U.S. non-farm payrolls data due at 1230 GMT. Weak jobs data could increase expectations for U.S. interest-rate cuts in the coming months, starting in September. They come after U.S. job openings data and ADP private payrolls data came in weaker than expected. "A reading around consensus would be a sign that the U.S. labor market is slowing down sharply," XTB's Kathleen Brooks says in a note. Gilts shrug off U.K. retail sales data showing a solid monthly rise during July but a weaker-than-expected annual increase. Ten-year gilt yields fall 3 basis points to last trade at 4.707%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0719 GMT - Jefferies' bigger worry about France is about ratings rather than about politics. Fitch Ratings is due to review France on Sept. 12, and it already has a negative outlook. "If political uncertainty persists, we see a case for Fitch downgrading France," Jefferies' global economist Mohit Kumar says in a note. However, Moody's Ratings or S&P Global Ratings are unlikely to downgrade France, according to Jefferies. "Risk remains that if France loses the coveted AA level rating (currently AA-) from two or more rating agencies, it could lead to forced selling from some of the Asian accounts." For now, Jefferies remains bearish on French bonds and expects the 10-year French-German bond-yield spread to rise towards 90 basis points, from 77 basis points currently, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0702 GMT - Sterling edges higher after data showed U.K. retail sales rose slightly more than expected during July. Retail sales increased 0.6% in July compared to the previous month, above the 0.5% forecast by economists in a WSJ survey. The data come after Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey said earlier in the week that the timing of future interest-rate cuts is now more uncertain amid concerns about inflationary pressures. The market is pricing in a 36% chance of another rate cut by year-end, LSEG data show. Sterling rises to an intraday high of $1.3471 after the data, from $1.3460 beforehand. The euro falls to an intraday low of 0.8662, pounds from 0.8673 previously. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0637 GMT - Nordic markets are seen opening slightly higher, with IG calling the OMXS30 up 0.3% at around 2615. The market is eagerly awaiting today's U.S. labor market data and how this will affect expectations of the Fed's interest-rate cuts, SEB's chief emerging markets strategist Erik Meyersson writes. SEB expects another month of employment growth of 75,000--in line with consensus--and unemployment to continue to rise slightly. "We think this will be enough for a 25 basis-point cut from the Fed in September." The money market is now pricing in at least two interest-rate cuts by the Fed before the end of the year, he says. OMXS30 closed at 2607.54, OMXN40 at 2293.02 and OBX at 1556.28. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0633 GMT - Eurozone government bond yields fall, tracking the move of U.S. Treasury yields and reflecting expctations of a further cooling U.S. labor market. U.S. payrolls are due at 1230 GMT, with analysts forecasting the addition of 75,000 jobs in August. Weak data is expected to cement interest-rate cut expectations by the Federal Reserve this month. "A September rate cut is likely a done deal," says J.P.Morgan Asset Management's Max McKechnie in a note. "Today's jobs report is more about the Federal Reserve's path from there," the global market strategist says. Eurozone 10-year bond yields drop by up to 4 basis points, with the 10-year German Bund yield falling 2.3 basis points to 2.701%, according to Tradeweb. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield falls 1 basis point to 3.580%. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0630 GMT - The dollar falls as markets await the highly-anticipated U.S. nonfarm payrolls report later for hints on the potential pace of expected Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts. Economists in a WSJ survey expect the report at 1230 GMT to show payrolls rose 75,000 in August. The unemployment rate is expected to rise to 4.3% in August from 4.2% previously and wage growth is forecast to slow to 3.8% year-on-year. With immigration slowing, modest jobs growth doesn't necessarily indicate a loosening labor market, J.P. Morgan Asset Management strategist Max McKechnie says in a note. "Instead, investors should focus on the unemployment rate and wage growth for a clearer sense of the Fed's next steps." The DXY dollar index falls 0.2% to 98.113. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0625 GMT - The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield "wants" to have a go at 4% amid expectations of a weak payroll outcome, ING's Padhraic Garvey and Benjamin Schroeder say in a note. August payrolls data due at 1230 GMT, could fortify expectations of a Federal Reserve interest-rate cut this month. "The 10-year [yield] looks to have 4% as a level to aim for, depending on the payrolls number," the rates strategists say. However, a job addition data below 50,000 would likely to be needed to generate that type of market reaction, they say. "The closer to zero [in job additions] the more likely a 4% test becomes," they say. Analysts in a WSJ poll expect 75,000 jobs added in August. The 10-year yield falls 2.3 basis points to 4.152%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0605 GMT - A Trump executive order locks in a 15% base tariff rate for Japan, clearing some uncertainty but leaving key questions unanswered, says Stefan Angrick at Moody's Analytics. The order settles some doubts over car duties and cements Japan's pledge to buy more U.S. rice. But investment provisions remain murky, the economist says. Japan will funnel $550 billion into the U.S., though the composition is unclear. U.S. Commerce Secretary Lutnick insists it's real funding, while Tokyo has said the figure includes loans, guarantees, and equity via government-backed institutions, with direct equity accounting for just 1% to 2%, Angrick says. Economically, tariffs remain punishingly high. They are undercutting manufacturing and exports, and will trim at least half a percentage point from GDP. Politically, the deal offers scant relief for beleaguered PM Ishiba. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0557 GMT - Southeast Asia is likely to continue taking a large share of global foreign direct investment flows, says HSBC economists in a commentary. While the Trump administration's demand for investment commitments from its trading partners could pull flows away from Southeast Asia, the region's economic competitiveness and Chinese businesses' need to diversify should still underpin the strong FDI flows, they say. They expect Southeast Asian economies to keep pursuing free-trade agreements, and their growing consumer markets and digital economies could offer investment opportunities beyond manufacturing. The momentum of outbound investment from China should also remain robust, with Southeast Asia likely to benefit, partly due to the region's geographic significance in China's Belt and Road Initiative, they add. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

data.title: Bitcoin Rises to One-Week High Before U.S. Jobs Data — Market Talk
data.description: Bitcoin rises to its highest in one week ahead of key U.S. jobs data at 1230 GMT as markets bet on the Federal Reserve resuming interest-rate cuts from the September 17 meeting. Weaker-than-expected ADP private payrolls data Thursday fuelled expectations for another soft nonfarm payroll report on Friday. This would strengthen the case for the Fed to cut rates later this month and likely beyond, potentially boosting risky assets including bitcoin. Bitcoin rises 2.1% to one-week high of $112,876, LSEG data show, extending gains after breaking back above $112,000. However, it remains well below the record high of $124,480 reached in mid-August, having recently fallen as traders took profits. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: US tariffs still spell big pain for Japan's small automakers
data.description:
Key points:
  • Smaller Japanese car companies face pressure to raise prices in key market
  • Mitsubishi faces biggest challenge with no North American production
  • US consumers may turn to used cars due to price hikes

President Donald Trump gave Japanese automakers some relief by cutting his high U.S. tariffs on their vehicles, but the reduced levies still mean big pain for Japan's smaller car companies, which will stay under pressure in the crucial market.

After months of diplomacy, Trump signed an order on Thursday cutting U.S. tariffs on imported Japanese autos to 15% from 27.5%, a rare bit of good news for Japan's critical industry, which relies on the United States as its top market.

Smaller automakers Mitsubishi Motors , Mazda and Subaru are less able to absorb the tariff shock than bigger rivals Toyota and Honda .

Mitsubishi, Mazda and Subaru, big exporters to the U.S., may need to keep raising prices - adding to an inflationary squeeze on U.S. consumers. Eventually the companies may need to pursue closer tie-ups with rivals, stop offering some models in the U.S. or even pull back from the market, analysts say.

Trump's 15% tariff on Japanese cars is still six times what the rate was, at 2.5%, before Trump slapped levies on autos in April.

Mitsubishi cut its profit forecast by nearly a third last week, and Mazda's exports from Mexico have sunk by more than half since Trump imposed auto tariffs.

The lower rates, which will take effect within a week or two, according to Japan's top tariff negotiator, do not apply to cars shipped to the U.S. from the key production hubs of Mexico and Canada. That means Japanese automakers could still face higher tariffs on vehicles from these countries, though cars qualified under a North American trade pact will be taxed only on their non-U.S. content.

Subaru sold 668,000 vehicles in the U.S. last year, Mazda 424,000 and Mitsubishi 110,000 - combined barely half of Toyota's 2.3 million.

HEAVY U.S. RELIANCE

Mitsubishi faces the biggest challenge because it does not produce cars in North America and relies on cheaper models that are more impacted by price hikes.

"If the cost of their vehicles goes up, then they lose the market advantage they have and could potentially retreat from the U.S. market," said Sam Fiorani, vice president at AutoForecast Solutions.

Last month, Mitsubishi Motors CEO Takao Kato said the company was considering the best approach for potential North American factory collaboration with Nissan following recent environmental regulation changes.

Tokyo-based Mitsubishi has raised prices the most among automakers in the U.S. since tariffs began, averaging $2,403 per vehicle, according to a July survey by online marketplace CarGurus. Subaru had the third-largest increase at $824, while many automakers cut prices.

Mazda's Mexico-to-U.S. shipments plunged 54% in the four months starting in April from the same period last year, by far the steepest drop among the 12 automakers that exported to the U.S. from its southern neighbour, Mexican government data shows.

Seeking a boost within the U.S., Mazda is increasing production of its CX-50 crossover SUV at a plant it operates with Toyota in Alabama. It has lowered U.S.-bound shipments from Mexico to preserve margins and has cut incentives on these models, Chief Executive Masahiro Moro said last month.

The Hiroshima-based automaker was likely hoping consumers would accept higher prices, said analyst Julie Boote at Pelham Smithers Associates.

She expects Mazda to work more closely with Toyota - such as by teaming up to make vehicles in the U.S. and on procurement and distribution - with Toyota raising its roughly 5% stake in the company within the next two years.

Mazda managed to maintain slight U.S. sales growth over the April-July period, helped by its move to step up U.S. production.

PRICE HIKES

U.S. consumers, facing higher prices for new cars, may choose used vehicles if new ones would blow their budgets.

"Automakers will start gradually raising prices by model, and new models will be priced higher than older ones," said Koji Endo, head of equities research at SBI Securities, indicating companies will try to minimise the impact on profits.

Mazda said last month it was raising the cost on the base version of its Mazda3 sedan to $24,550 for the 2026 model year, up only $400 from this year, but nearly 20% higher than in 2022.

Subaru decided to eliminate the base version of its Outback model for next year, after unveiling price hikes on several models in response to "market conditions" in May.

Mitsubishi also faces the difficult choice of absorbing the tariff costs or passing them to consumers, risking further loss of market share.

U.S. consumers may increasingly be pushed into the used-car market, Fiorani warned.

"When you get a buyer who's looking for a $30,000 CX-30 or a Mitsubishi Outlander, there's not a lot of competition at their price point," he said.

"It's going to force them to buy a three-year-old used crossover."

data.title: Gold Edges Higher After Record Run as Traders Await U.S. Jobs Data — Market Talk
data.description: Gold prices edge higher in early trade after pausing from a record-breaking rally as traders locked in profits ahead of the release of key U.S. nonfarm payrolls data. Futures are up 0.1% to $3,610.40 a troy ounce and on track for a 2.6% weekly gain, after hitting a new high of $3,640.10/oz earlier this week. The precious metal's surge was largely driven by expectations that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates at its next meeting, concerns about the U.S. central bank's independence following President Trump's move to fire Fed governor Lisa Cook, and persistent geopolitical risks. Now, "the biggest risk from a market perspective is an upside surprise to today's payrolls number," says Kathleen Brooks, research director at XTB. "If rate cut bets get scaled back, then inflation fears could ease, which may hurt the world's most famous inflation hedge." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: European Stocks Cautious, US Jobs Awaited
data.description:
European stocks were cautious on Friday, with the STOXX 50 and STOXX 600 both swinging around the flatline, ahead of the closely watched US jobs report, which could shape expectations for a potential Fed rate cut later this month.

In macro news, German factory orders fell 2.9% in July, the steepest drop since January and a reminder of persistent weakness in industrial demand.

On the corporate front, ASML gained over 2%, while Safran rose 1% after reports it is exploring the sale of much of its aircraft interiors division.

Orsted added nearly 1%, despite cutting its 2025 operating profit outlook, just ahead of a shareholder vote on a key capital increase.

Meanwhile, SAP (-0.5%), LVMH (-0.5%), Novo Nordisk (-0.2%), and Nestlé (-0.4%) were among the notable decliners.

    Irrevocable commitments and letters of intent should not be included.  If there are no such agreements, arrangements or understandings, state "none"

(b)        Agreements, arrangements or understandings relating to options or derivatives

  • Details of any agreement, arrangement or understanding, formal or informal, between the exempt principal trader making the disclosure and any other person relating to:

    (i)  the voting rights of any relevant securities under any option; or

    (ii) the voting rights or future acquisition or disposal of any relevant securities to which any derivative is referenced:

    If there are no such agreements, arrangements or understandings, state "none"

  • NONE

(c)        Attachments

data.title: DAX on Cautious Trading
data.description:
The DAX in Frankfurt traded cautiously on Friday, edging up 0.1% to around 23,790, in line with its peers, in line with its peers, as investors awaited the highly anticipated U.S. jobs report for August, due later in the day.

Signs of a further slowdown in the labor market appear increasingly likely, following weaker-than-expected private sector data released on Thursday—reinforcing expectations of a potential Fed rate cut within the next two weeks.

Meanwhile, traders also weighed the implications of Porsche AG’s upcoming exit from Germany’s DAX index on September 22, 2025.

The decision, prompted by a 30% year-to-date decline in the company’s stock and ongoing structural headwinds—including U.S. tariffs on European imports and sluggish demand in China—highlights deeper vulnerabilities within the German automotive sector.

data.title: Copper rises on China supply concerns; investors eye US jobs data
data.description:
Copper prices posted modest gains on Friday, supported by a rare decline in refined copper production in top producer China, while investors kept an eye on key U.S. jobs data.

Three-month copper on the London Metal Exchange was up 0.55% at $9,952.5 per metric ton, as of 0706 GMT, and on track to end the week 0.54% higher, if the current trend holds.

The most-traded copper contract on the Shanghai Futures Exchange gained 0.2% to 80,140 yuan ($11,203.85) a ton, ending the week 0.91% higher.

China's refined copper production is forecast for a rare fall this month amid newly introduced tax regulations to constrain the supply of scrap copper.

Despite sluggish operating rates and weaker-than-expected imports driven by shifts in scrap copper policy, analysts from Everbright Futures expect a strong rebound in refined copper consumption.

Meanwhile, BYD , , the world's largest electric vehicle manufacturer, slashed its sales target for the year by as much as 16% to 4.6 million vehicles from its estimate of 5.5 million vehicles in March.

Copper is widely used in electric vehicles and charging infrastructure.

On the other hand, the dollar index , which measures the U.S. currency against six major peers, dipped 0.1% to 98.14, though it was up 0.4% for the week.

A weaker dollar makes greenback-denominated assets like copper more affordable to holders of other currencies.

Focus will also be on the U.S. non-farm payrolls data, due at 1230 GMT, that could offer more clarity on the Fed's interest rate trajectory.

Among other London metals, aluminium rose 0.46%, lead climbed 0.18%, tin gained 0.23%, zinc increased 0.53%, and nickel was up 0.03%.

SHFE aluminium edged 0.29% higher, lead rose 0.21%, zinc increased 0.09%, while nickel dipped 0.16%, and tin lost 0.12%.
